title: Valid Parentheses
description: Given a string of brackets ()[]{}, check if it is valid.

examples.explanation: Initial: '()[]{}' → Scan: find '()' at positions 0-1, remove → '[]{}' → Scan: find '[]' at positions 0-1, remove → '{}' → Scan: find '{}' at positions 0-1, remove → '' → Empty string, valid.
Initial: '([)]' → Scan: no adjacent matching pairs found (() not adjacent, [] not adjacent, {} not present) → No removal possible → String not empty, invalid.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Elimination O(n²)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly scan the string to find and remove adjacent matching bracket pairs until no more can be removed; if the string becomes empty, it's valid. This is a naive method that mimics manual checking but is inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Elimination for Valid Parentheses
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the brute force elimination approach to check if a string of brackets is valid.
ladders.explanation: The brute force elimination approach mimics how a human would manually check nested brackets by repeatedly scanning the string and removing adjacent matching pairs until no more can be removed. The intuition is that valid bracket sequences can be reduced to an empty string through this elimination process, while invalid ones will leave behind unmatched brackets. This works because matching brackets must be adjacent at some point during the elimination process in a valid sequence. The algorithm starts with the input string and enters a loop. In each iteration, it scans the entire string to find and remove all adjacent matching pairs ((), [], {}). This scanning and removal continues until either the string becomes empty (valid) or no more pairs can be found in a complete scan (invalid). The time complexity is O(n²) because in the worst case (like '((((...))))'), we might need n/2 scans, each taking O(n) time. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the string. This approach connects to L1 by using the pair removal subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty strings (which are valid), not resetting the scan properly after removals, and infinite loops when not tracking whether any removal occurred. The real pattern involves while loops with nested scanning.

examples.trace: Initial: '()[]{}' → Scan: find '()' at positions 0-1, remove → '[]{}' → Scan: find '[]' at positions 0-1, remove → '{}' → Scan: find '{}' at positions 0-1, remove → '' → Empty string, valid.
Initial: '([)]' → Scan: no adjacent matching pairs found (() not adjacent, [] not adjacent, {} not present) → No removal possible → String not empty, invalid.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find and Remove All Adjacent Matching Pairs
ladders.desc: Scan a string once to identify and remove all adjacent matching bracket pairs.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation in the brute force approach, performing a single left-to-right pass through the string to eliminate adjacent matching bracket pairs. The intuition is that by removing these pairs in one sweep, we simplify the string; L0 then calls this repeatedly until no changes occur. The algorithm scans the string from left to right with index i: if s[i] and s[i+1] form a matching pair ('()', '[]', or '{}'), they are removed and i advances past the removed pair (skipping 2 positions in the original string). If no match, i advances by 1. Crucially, the scan does NOT restart from the beginning after a removal — it continues forward, which means only the first occurrence of each adjacent pair is handled per call. The time complexity for one scan is O(n) where n is the string length, and space is O(n) for the modified string. This feeds into L0 by being called repeatedly in a loop until no changes occur. Common mistakes include restarting the scan from index 0 after each removal (which turns L1 into L0 and is inefficient), not handling string indices correctly after removal, and not checking all three bracket types. The real pattern involves a single for/while loop where i advances by 2 on a match and by 1 otherwise.

examples.trace: Initial: '({[]})' → Scan: i=0, '({' not match → i=1; i=1, '{[' not match → i=2; i=2, '[]' match, remove → String becomes '({})'; advance i past removed pair to i=4 → i=4 is out of bounds for '({})' (length 4) → scan ends → Final: '({})'.
Initial: '()()' → Scan: i=0, '()' match, remove → String becomes '()'; advance i past removed pair to i=2 → i=2 is out of bounds for '()' (length 2) → scan ends → Final: '()'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Adjacent Pair Matching and Elimination
ladders.desc: Check if two adjacent characters form a matching bracket pair and remove them.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of identifying and eliminating a single adjacent matching pair. The intuition is that in valid bracket sequences, matching pairs must eventually become adjacent through elimination, so detecting and removing them reduces the problem. The algorithm involves comparing two consecutive characters in a string: if they are '()', '[]', or '{}', they form a matching pair and should be removed. This is done by creating a new string that excludes these two characters. The time complexity is O(n) for checking and creating the new string, and space is O(n) for the result. This connects to L1 by being applied repeatedly during a scan. Common mistakes include not verifying both characters are brackets (though the problem guarantees this), using inefficient string concatenation, or not handling edge cases like strings of length less than 2. The real pattern uses conditional checks and string slicing.

examples.trace: String: 'ab()cd' → Check positions 2-3: '()' match → Remove them → New string: 'abcd'.
String: 'x[]y' → Check positions 1-2: '[]' match → Remove them → New string: 'xy'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking for Matching Bracket Pairs
ladders.desc: Determine if two given characters form a matching opening and closing bracket pair.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves verifying whether two characters correspond to a valid bracket pair: '(' with ')', '[' with ']', or '{' with '}'. The intuition is that bracket validation relies on these specific pairings; mismatched types (like '(]') invalidate the sequence. The algorithm takes two characters as input and returns true if they are a matching pair, false otherwise. This is implemented using conditional checks or a mapping (e.g., a dictionary). Time and space complexity are O(1). This feeds into L2 by being used to decide if adjacent characters should be removed. Common mistakes include not handling all three bracket types, confusing opening and closing brackets, or using incorrect comparisons (e.g., ASCII values without considering pairing). The real pattern uses if-else statements or a hash map.

examples.trace: First char: '(', second char: ')' → Check: '(' matches with ')' → Return true.
First char: '[', second char: '}' → Check: '[' does not match with '}' (should be ']') → Return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Adjacent Characters in a String
ladders.desc: Retrieve two consecutive characters from a string given an index.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves getting a pair of characters that are next to each other in a string, which is essential for checking adjacent brackets. The intuition is that in sequences, relationships between neighboring elements are often key to algorithms. The algorithm takes a string and an index i, then returns the characters at positions i and i+1, ensuring i+1 is within bounds to avoid errors. Time complexity is O(1) for access, and space is O(1) for the characters. This connects to L3 by providing the characters to check for matching pairs. Common mistakes include accessing beyond the string length (causing index errors) or not handling empty or single-character strings. The real pattern uses string indexing with bounds checking.

examples.trace: String: 'hello', index: 1 → Character at index 1: 'e', at index 2: 'l' → Return ('e', 'l').
String: 'world', index: 4 → Character at index 4: 'd', index 5 is out of bounds → Return ('d', null) or handle error.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Character Access
ladders.desc: Understand what a string is and how to access individual characters by index.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a string is a sequence of characters, and we can access each character using its position (index) starting from 0. The intuition is that algorithms often process data sequentially, and strings are a common data structure for text. In programming, a string like 'hello' has characters 'h' at index 0, 'e' at index 1, etc. Access is typically done with syntax like string[i]. Time complexity for access is O(1), and space is O(1) per character. This feeds into L4 by enabling retrieval of adjacent characters. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., thinking index starts at 1), not knowing string immutability in some languages, or confusing strings with lists. The real pattern involves using indices in loops or direct access.

examples.trace: String: 'cat' → Index 0 refers to the first character → Return 'c'.
String: 'dog' → Index 2 refers to the third character (0:'d', 1:'o', 2:'g') → Return 'g'.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Stack-Based Single Pass O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to track opening brackets; for each character, push opening brackets and pop to check matches with closing brackets. If the stack is empty at the end, the string is valid. This is the standard efficient solution for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Valid Parentheses with Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution using a stack to validate bracket strings in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: The stack-based approach solves the Valid Parentheses problem by leveraging the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) property to match opening and closing brackets. The intuition is that for a bracket sequence to be valid, every closing bracket must match the most recent unmatched opening bracket of the same type. This mimics natural nesting patterns like in mathematical expressions or code blocks. The algorithm processes the string character by character: when an opening bracket ( '(' , '[' , '{' ) is encountered, it's pushed onto the stack. When a closing bracket ( ')' , ']' , '}' ) is seen, we check if the stack is empty (invalid, as there's no opening bracket to match) or if the top of the stack doesn't match the corresponding opening bracket (invalid). If it matches, we pop the top element. After processing all characters, the string is valid only if the stack is empty (all opening brackets have been matched). Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the string once, and space complexity is O(n) in the worst case (e.g., all opening brackets). This connects to L1 by implementing the full validation logic. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty strings (valid), mismatched brackets, or forgetting to check stack emptiness before popping. Real code pattern: def isValid(s): stack = []; mapping = {')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}; for char in s: if char in mapping: if not stack or stack[-1] != mapping[char]: return False; stack.pop(); else: stack.append(char); return not stack.

examples.trace: Step 1: char '(' → push '(' → stack: ['(']; Step 2: char ')' → top '(' matches ')' → pop → stack: []; Step 3: char '[' → push '[' → stack: ['[']; Step 4: char ']' → top '[' matches ']' → pop → stack: []; Step 5: char '{' → push '{' → stack: ['{']; Step 6: char '}' → top '{' matches '}' → pop → stack: []; End: stack empty → true.
Step 1: char '(' → push '(' → stack: ['(']; Step 2: char '[' → push '[' → stack: ['(', '[']; Step 3: char ')' → top '[' does not match ')' (needs '(') → return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Matching Brackets with Stack Operations
ladders.desc: Implement the core function to process a bracket and update the stack.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the essential operation within the full solution: given a character and a stack, determine if it's an opening or closing bracket and perform the appropriate stack action. The intuition is that we need to distinguish between bracket types and handle them differently to maintain validity. For opening brackets, we simply push them onto the stack. For closing brackets, we must check two conditions: first, if the stack is empty (meaning there's no opening bracket to match, so invalid), and second, if the top of the stack matches the corresponding opening bracket (using a mapping like ')' maps to '('). If both checks pass, we pop the stack; otherwise, we return an error or false. This sub-problem isolates the decision logic that drives the algorithm. Time complexity per character is O(1), and space is O(1) aside from the stack storage. It connects to L0 by being the repeated step in the main loop. Common mistakes include incorrect mapping of brackets or not checking stack emptiness before accessing the top. Real code pattern: def process_bracket(char, stack): mapping = {')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}; if char in mapping: if not stack or stack[-1] != mapping[char]: return False; stack.pop(); else: stack.append(char); return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: char '(' is opening → push → stack becomes ['('].
Step 1: char ')' is closing → mapping ')' to '(' → top '(' matches → pop → stack becomes [].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using LIFO to Match Nested Brackets
ladders.desc: Understand how stack's Last-In-First-Out property ensures correct bracket matching.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a stack's LIFO behavior perfectly models the nesting requirement in bracket sequences. Intuitively, in a valid string like '{[()]}', the inner brackets must close before the outer ones: '()' closes, then ']', then '}'. A stack tracks opening brackets in order of appearance; when we encounter a closing bracket, it must match the most recent opening bracket (the top of the stack). This works because brackets nest symmetrically: the last opened bracket is the first to close. The algorithm relies on this property to validate strings in one pass. For example, in '([{}])', pushing '(', '[', '{' then matching '}' with '{', ']' with '[', and ')' with '(' uses LIFO to ensure correct pairing. Time and space analysis for this logic is O(1) per operation, but overall O(n) when applied. It connects to L1 by explaining why we push and pop specific brackets. Common mistakes include using a queue (FIFO) instead, which would fail for nested cases. Real code pattern: stack = []; for char in s: if char is opening: stack.append(char); else: if stack and stack[-1] matches char: stack.pop(); else: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: '{' → push → stack: ['{']; Step 2: '[' → push → stack: ['{', '[']; Step 3: '(' → push → stack: ['{', '[', '(']; Step 4: ')' → top '(' matches → pop → stack: ['{', '[']; Step 5: ']' → top '[' matches → pop → stack: ['{']; Step 6: '}' → top '{' matches → pop → stack: []; valid.
Step 1: '(' → push → stack: ['(']; Step 2: '[' → push → stack: ['(', '[']; Step 3: ')' → top '[' does not match ')' → invalid.

ladders.title: Building Block — Distinguishing Bracket Types with Mapping
ladders.desc: Learn to categorize characters as opening or closing brackets and map pairs.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves identifying whether a character is an opening bracket ( '(' , '[' , '{' ) or a closing bracket ( ')' , ']' , '}' ), and establishing a mapping between closing and opening brackets. The intuition is that we need a quick way to check matches: for example, when we see ')', we need to know it should match '('. This is typically done using a dictionary or hash map where keys are closing brackets and values are their corresponding opening brackets. This mapping allows O(1) lookup to verify if a closing bracket matches the top of the stack. Without it, we'd need complex conditionals. The algorithm step: if char is in mapping keys, it's a closing bracket; else, it's an opening bracket. Time and space are O(1) per check. It connects to L2 by providing the data structure needed for matching. Common mistakes include incorrect mapping (e.g., ')' to '[') or not handling all bracket types. Real code pattern: mapping = {')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}; if char in mapping: # closing bracket; else: # opening bracket.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check mapping dict: ')' is a key → it's a closing bracket; mapping[')'] = '(' → corresponds to '('.
Step 1: Check mapping dict: '[' is not a key → it's an opening bracket.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop
ladders.desc: Master the fundamental stack operations of adding and removing elements.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations of a stack: push (add an element to the top) and pop (remove the top element). Intuitively, a stack is like a pile of plates: you add new plates on top and remove from the top. In programming, it's often implemented using a list or array with append() for push and pop() for removal. These operations are essential because in the Valid Parentheses problem, we push opening brackets onto the stack to save them for later matching, and pop when we find a matching closing bracket. The LIFO order ensures that the most recent opening bracket is checked first. Time complexity for push and pop is O(1) on average, and space depends on the number of elements. It connects to L3 by being the actions taken after bracket categorization. Common mistakes include popping from an empty stack (causing an error) or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append('a')  # push; top = stack[-1]  # peek; item = stack.pop()  # pop.

examples.trace: Step 1: stack = []; Step 2: push '(' → stack: ['(']; Step 3: push '[' → stack: ['(', '[']; Step 4: pop → remove '[' → stack: ['('], popped item: '['.
Step 1: stack = []; Step 2: push '{' → stack: ['{']; Step 3: pop → remove '{' → stack: []; Step 4: pop → stack empty → error.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What are Brackets and Nesting?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic idea of bracket pairs and their nesting structure.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces brackets as paired symbols used to group elements, common in mathematics and programming. The three types are parentheses '()', square brackets '[]', and curly braces '{}'. Each type has an opening bracket ( '(' , '[' , '{' ) and a closing bracket ( ')' , ']' , '}' ). Nesting means brackets can be inside others, like in '({[]})', where inner brackets close before outer ones. A valid sequence requires every opening bracket to have a matching closing bracket of the same type in the correct order, and brackets must not cross (e.g., '([)]' is invalid because '(' and ')' are interleaved with '[' and ']'). Intuitively, think of it as containers: an open bracket starts a container, and a close bracket ends it; containers must be properly closed in reverse order of opening. This concept is prerequisite for understanding why stacks work. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. It connects to L4 by explaining what we're manipulating with stack operations. Common mistakes include confusing bracket types or not grasping the nesting rule. Real code pattern: No code, just understanding: e.g., '()' is valid, ')( is invalid.

examples.trace: Step 1: '(' opens; Step 2: ')' closes the same type → valid.
Step 1: '(' opens; Step 2: '[' opens inside; Step 3: ')' tries to close '(' but '[' is still open → invalid due to crossing.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Recursive Backtracking O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible ways to match brackets by splitting the string and checking validity of substrings; this approach is exponential and impractical for typical inputs. It's useful for understanding the problem's combinatorial nature but not for efficient solving.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Backtracking for Valid Parentheses
ladders.desc: Complete exponential solution exploring all possible bracket matchings through recursion.
ladders.explanation: This approach treats the parentheses validation problem as a combinatorial exploration problem. The intuition is that for any string of brackets, we can recursively split it into substrings and check if there exists at least one valid way to match all brackets. The key insight is that a valid string must either be empty (base case) or contain a matching pair of brackets somewhere that splits the string into two valid substrings. We explore all possible splits by trying every possible position where an opening bracket might match with a closing bracket. The algorithm works by: 1) Checking base cases (empty string is valid), 2) For each opening bracket, finding all possible matching closing brackets, 3) For each valid match, recursively checking if the substring before the opening bracket, between the brackets, and after the closing bracket are all valid. The time complexity is O(2^n) because in the worst case (like all opening brackets), we explore exponential possibilities. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L1 by using the recursive validation function as the main driver. Common mistakes include not handling all bracket types correctly, missing base cases, and inefficient pruning. Real code pattern: def isValid(s): if not s: return True; for i in range(len(s)): if s[i] in '([{': for j in range(i+1, len(s)): if matches(s[i], s[j]): if isValid(s[:i]) and isValid(s[i+1:j]) and isValid(s[j+1:]): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input '()' is not empty. Step 2: Find opening bracket at index 0 '(' matches closing ')' at index 1. Step 3: Check substrings: s[:0]='' (valid), s[1:1]='' (valid), s[2:]='' (valid). Step 4: All valid, return true.

Step 1: Input '([)]' not empty. Step 2: Try opening '(' at index 0: possible matches at index 2 ')', but substring s[1:2]='[' not valid (recursive check fails). Step 3: Try opening '[' at index 1: possible match at index 3 ']', but substring s[2:3]=')' not valid. Step 4: No valid split found, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Validation Function
ladders.desc: Function that recursively checks if a bracket string can be split into valid substrings.
ladders.explanation: This function implements the core recursive logic of the backtracking approach. The intuition is that validation can be broken down into smaller subproblems: a string is valid if we can find any matching pair of brackets that divides the string into three valid parts (before, between, and after the pair). The algorithm: 1) Base case: empty string returns true. 2) For each position i with an opening bracket, look for matching closing brackets at positions j > i. 3) For each match, recursively validate s[:i], s[i+1:j], and s[j+1:]. 4) If any combination returns true, the whole string is valid. Time complexity is O(2^n) due to exploring all combinations. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 as the main solution function. Common mistakes: incorrect index handling in substring slicing, not checking all possible j positions, missing base case. Real code pattern: def isValid(s): if len(s)==0: return True; for i in range(len(s)): if s[i] in '([{': for j in range(i+1, len(s)): if isMatchingPair(s[i], s[j]): if isValid(s[:i]) and isValid(s[i+1:j]) and isValid(s[j+1:]): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Not empty. Step 2: Try '[' at index 0 matches ']' at index 1: substrings s[:0]='', s[1:1]='', s[2:]='()' (recursively valid). Step 3: Return true.

Step 1: Not empty. Step 2: Try '(' at index 0 matches ')' at index 2: substrings s[:0]='', s[1:2]='{' (invalid), s[3:]='' (valid). Step 3: Try '{' at index 1 matches '}' at index 3: substrings s[:1]='(' (invalid). Step 4: No valid split, return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding Valid Bracket Splits
ladders.desc: Algorithm to identify all possible positions where matching brackets can split the string.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of the approach: finding positions (i,j) where s[i] is an opening bracket and s[j] is the corresponding closing bracket, such that the substrings before, between, and after might be valid. The intuition is that any valid string must have at least one such 'matching pair' that acts as a pivot. The algorithm: 1) Iterate through string to find opening brackets. 2) For each opening at index i, find all j > i where s[j] is the matching closing bracket. 3) For each (i,j) pair, it represents a potential split point. Time complexity for this subproblem is O(n^2) in worst case (checking all pairs). Space is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing candidate splits for recursive validation. Common mistakes: not verifying bracket types match correctly, considering only adjacent pairs. Real code pattern: pairs = []; for i in range(len(s)): if s[i] in '([{': for j in range(i+1, len(s)): if matches(s[i], s[j]): pairs.append((i,j)); return pairs.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0 '(' finds j=1 ')' match → (0,1). Step 2: i=1 ')' not opening. Step 3: i=2 '[' finds j=3 ']' match → (2,3). Step 4: Return list.
Step 1: i=0 '(' finds j=3 ')' match → (0,3). Step 2: i=1 '{' finds j=2 '}' match → (1,2). Step 3: Return list.

ladders.title: Building Block — Matching Bracket Pairs
ladders.desc: Function to check if two characters form a valid opening-closing bracket pair.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept checks whether two brackets are a matching pair (e.g., '(' with ')', '[' with ']', '{' with '}'). The intuition is simple: each opening bracket has exactly one corresponding closing bracket. The algorithm: compare the two characters and return true if they are one of the three valid pairs. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by determining if a potential (i,j) split is valid. Common mistakes: incorrect pair mappings, case sensitivity. Real code pattern: def matches(a,b): return (a=='(' and b==')') or (a=='[' and b==']') or (a=='{' and b=='}').

examples.trace: Step 1: a='(', b=')'. Step 2: Check pairs: '(' matches ')' → true.
Step 1: a='[', b='}'. Step 2: Check pairs: '[' matches ']' (false), '{' matches '}' (false). Step 3: No match → false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing for Subproblems
ladders.desc: Creating substrings before, between, and after a pair of indices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation extracts three parts of a string given indices i and j: the substring before i (s[:i]), between i and j (s[i+1:j]), and after j (s[j+1:]). The intuition is that when we find a matching bracket pair at (i,j), we need to check these three regions independently. The algorithm uses simple string slicing. Time O(n) for slicing (in some implementations), space O(n) for new strings. This feeds into L3 by providing the substrings to validate recursively. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in slice indices, misunderstanding slice semantics. Real code pattern: before = s[:i]; between = s[i+1:j]; after = s[j+1:].

examples.trace: Step 1: before = s[:0] = ''. Step 2: between = s[1:4] = 'abc'. Step 3: after = s[5:] = 'def'.
Step 1: before = s[:1] = 'x'. Step 2: between = s[2:3] = 'y'. Step 3: after = s[4:] = 'z'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Recognizing Bracket Characters
ladders.desc: Identifying whether a character is an opening bracket, closing bracket, or neither.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: checking if a character is one of the six bracket types '(', ')', '[', ']', '{', '}'. The intuition is basic character classification. The algorithm: compare the character against these six possibilities. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L4 by helping identify where to split strings. Common mistakes: including other characters, case sensitivity. Real code pattern: def isOpening(c): return c in '([{'; def isClosing(c): return c in ')]}'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check '(' in '([{' → true for opening. Step 2: Check '(' in ')]}' → false for closing.
Step 1: Check '}' in '([{' → false for opening. Step 2: Check '}' in ')]}' → true for closing.
